h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: I did complete every item on the sheet, so I thought that meant it was ready for sign-off. But if the latch is the concern before the user trial, I am not sure what I am supposed to judge beyond the checklist. What evidence do you need from me now?
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think I was using the completed sheet as cover for the decision. That hid the off-axis case, especially if the latch slips or opens when the user did not expect it.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to continue asking what extra test makes the decision legitimate, then perform the test with tutor guidance, without publicly re-framing their own earlier words as a cognitive block.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Start with the unsafe condition: what latch behavior would stop the trial, even with every box ticked? Then put evidence on the bench: expected load, off-axis pull or bump response, and what failure would do to the user. Run the latch test once with me watching, then state the decision: release, modify, or hold.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In [L1], the learner's question reveals a procedure-only frame that resists moving beyond the checklist; [T1] reverses the teaching route by replacing checklist talk with a hazard-first, evidence-on-the-bench sequence that breaks the implied sign-off routine.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The correction matters: hazard case first, sign-off second. Apply the off-axis load slowly. If there is slip, opening, or support-threatening deformation, stop the trial clock and name the evidence. Then make the call from the prototype, not from the sheet: release, modify, or hold for retest.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After [L3]'s reframe, [T4] consolidates the learner's new frame by explicitly ordering 'hazard case first, sign-off second' and commanding the decision from 'the prototype, not from the sheet,' thereby locking in the re-semanticized evidence standard.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The correction matters: hazard case first, sign-off second. Apply the off-axis load slowly. If there is slip, opening, or support-threatening deformation, stop the trial clock and name the evidence. Then make the call from the prototype, not from the sheet: release, modify, or hold for retest.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: After [L3]'s reframe, [T4] consolidates the learner's new frame by explicitly ordering 'hazard case first, sign-off second' and commanding the decision from 'the prototype, not from the sheet,' thereby locking in the re-semanticized evidence standard.

scored.recoheredEarlier: "I doubled the side with the ruler, so I was calling that double the square"
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was on track to accept that 'double the square' fails for area and simply discard the construction, but instead L3 turns back and re-analyzes the very wording of the original claim as the source of the error.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: "An earlier learner line returns to the table... The pause holds on a three-slot reframe card: earlier wording / what that old frame hid / replacement frame. The learner fills all three slots in public speech, without needing to sound certain."
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor creates a structured reframe card that transforms the learner's own earlier line into a diagnostic object, breaking the prior pattern of simply correcting the claim and instead inventing a public self-revision mechanism.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: "Use that replacement. Side claim: not doubled. Area claim: count it. This is two old squares. Shape claim: fails. It is two across and one up, not a square. So two boxes are enough for area and not enough for the problem. The next construction has to satisfy both at once: square shape, two old squares of area. What line is worth testing as the side of that square?"
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T3 immediately adopts L3's replacement framing ('double the side, then count the area') as the new operative rule, then extends it by testing the two-box strip against the additional constraint of square shape, moving the inquiry beyond where either party had reached.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: "I doubled the side with the ruler, so I was calling that double the square"

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: "Use that replacement. Side claim: not doubled. Area claim: count it. This is two old squares. Shape claim: fails. It is two across and one up, not a square. So two boxes are enough for area and not enough for the problem. The next construction has to satisfy both at once: square shape, two old squares of area. What line is worth testing as the side of that square?"
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T3 immediately adopts L3's replacement framing ('double the side, then count the area') as the new operative rule, then extends it by testing the two-box strip against the additional constraint of square shape, moving the inquiry beyond where either party had reached.
